author: @fishyerik
text:
Yeah, the moisture doesn't turn into power, it's does what the potato does in a potato battery. And, if you get <1 volt, and a few micro amps, for a short time, well, you need millions and millions times that just to charge a phone. It would be orders of magnitude easier to make something practically useful using TEGs and body heat as the heat source, or evaporative cooling to create the temperature difference. I'm not saying that TEGs are a good way to produce meaningful power from small temperature differences, they are not, but compared to "humidity power" they're fantastic.     

Just the basic idea that a state, like a level of air humidity, as this basic concept often is described, can be a source of energy, is fundamentally flawed, you need a difference, a potential, to extract energy from, which is mentioned in this video. But, you need that potential, if you try to scavenge power with you wall papers you either need moisture to travel through them, or have significant changes in air humidity, neither is very difficult to achieve, but neither is also something you'd want, and definitely not worth a few watts max generation from your whole home.    

The amount of power required to barely run a modern simple calculator is incredibly small. I can agree that "trivial" isn't a good description, as calling it trivial would not make it clear just how ridiculous small amount of power that requires. There were commercially produced calculators decades ago that was powered just by few square centimeters of solar cells, that didn't require very strong light to produce more power than the calculators needed. Now solar cells are about twice as efficient, and practically free, and there are much more energy efficient components now.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: I initially thought the same... but after reviewing the paper, they aren't using electrodes that will react chemically with the paper. So, there is something else going on... perhaps a reaction of the acid with the paper.

replies.author: @fishyerik
@marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @fishyerikYes, I agree with you there. It's clearly not just "plain" water... although there is a small initial reaction with plain water, after that it seems like acid water does the trick on a regular basis -- see Fig. 1 of the paper which shows repeated waveforms of electricity when drops of acid water are dripped onto the paper cells.

author: @Delchursing
text: Would vinegar work as the acid?

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: Yes... the paper describes using Acetic acid as well.

author: @jasonburguess
text: I love these videos, but in the off grid world miliamps is somewhat of a four letter word, I'd love to see a video about how to produce some serious power, say 10kw at 240 volts continuous.. small power is great if your needs are already met, but I've got a 30amp 240 vac well pump to run and a welder and a water heater and lots more, solar is great but in alaska in winter it's not as much as we need, anything to help us not need to run the petrol hungry 10kw generator that uses a u.s. gallon per hour (3.78liters) . Hopefully you may have some ideas and can make a video about them. Keep up the great work and merry Christmas from alaska!
